title: 第四周WiFi Protected Access (WPA)

slides.title: Lecture 10: Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA)
slides.text:
Lecture 10: Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA)
Instructor:
Kui
Ren
Wireless network and IoT security

slides.title: Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA) is
lines: A response by the WLAN industry to offer an immediate, a stronger security solution than WEP.
A software/firmware upgrade to existing access points
Inexpensive in terms of time and cost to implement
Suitable for enterprise, small sites, home networks
slides.text:
Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA) is
A response by the WLAN industry to offer an immediate, a stronger security solution than WEP.
WPA is intended to be:
A software/firmware upgrade to existing access points
Inexpensive in terms of time and cost to implement
Compatible with vendors
Suitable for enterprise, small sites, home networks
Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA)

slides.title: WPA wraps RC4 cipher engine in four new algorithms
lines: WPA wraps RC4 cipher engine in four new algorithms
A Message Integrity Code (MIC) called Michael
Extended 48-bit IV and IV Sequencing Rules
is a large number! More than 500 trillion
Sequencing rules specify how IVs are selected and verified
Creating a key hierarchy out of the master key
Master keys can be refreshed in a secure manner
Temporal Key Integrity Protocol generates per-packet keys
How WPA Addresses the WEP Vulnerabilities
slides.text:
WPA wraps RC4 cipher engine in four new algorithms
A Message Integrity Code (MIC) called Michael
Designed for deployed hardware
Requires use of active countermeasures
Extended 48-bit IV and IV Sequencing Rules
2
48
is a large number! More than 500 trillion
Sequencing rules specify how IVs are selected and verified
Key Derivation and Distribution
Creating a key hierarchy out of the master key
Master keys can be refreshed in a secure manner
Temporal Key Integrity Protocol generates per-packet keys
How WPA Addresses the WEP Vulnerabilities

Key WPA Facts

slides.title: Message Integrity Code (MIC) - 64-bit message calculated using the “Michael” algorithm
lines: Message Integrity Code (MIC) - 64-bit message calculated using the “Michael” algorithm
The Michael algorithm was the strongest that WPA designers could come up with that would still work with most older network cards
Uses a lot swaps, shifts, even discarding operations
The MIC is a hash that is calculated on a per-packet basis
The MIC is also on a per-sender, per-receiver basis
The MIC is based on seed value (IVs), destination MAC, source MAC, and payload
slides.text:
Message Integrity Code (MIC) - 64-bit message calculated using the “Michael” algorithm
Designed for deployed legacy hardware
The Michael algorithm was the strongest that WPA designers could come up with that would still work with most older network cards
Uses a lot swaps, shifts, even discarding operations
Protects both data and header
The MIC is a hash that is calculated on a per-packet basis
The MIC is also on a per-sender, per-receiver basis
The MIC is based on seed value (IVs), destination MAC, source MAC, and payload
Message Integrity Check: Michael Algorithm

slides.title: Due to inevitable weaknesses of Michael, WPA implements
lines: Due to inevitable weaknesses of Michael, WPA implements
Take the 1st and 3rd bytes of the old IV
Reset packet sequence # to 0 on rekey
Increment sequence # by 1 on each packet
Drop any packet received out of sequence
slides.text:
Due to inevitable weaknesses of Michael, WPA implements
a 16-bit monotonically incrementing counter
that discourages replay attacks:
Take the 1st and 3rd bytes of the old IV
Reset packet sequence # to 0 on rekey
Increment sequence # by 1 on each packet
Drop any packet received out of sequence
Defeating Replays: IV Sequence Enforcement

Session keys, collectively called the

(PTK), are derived from the master key

The mixing phases make difficult for an attacker to correlate IVs and per-packet key

TKIP
constructs a per-packet key by going through 2 key mixing phases
The mixing phases make difficult for an attacker to correlate IVs and per-packet key

slides.title: TKIP appears to provide weak but genuine security
lines: TKIP appears to provide weak but genuine security
, David Wagner, John Kelsey, Susan Langford, and others
TKIP meets goal of software deployment on almost all existing equipment
Does not appear to  significantly degrade performance over WEP
Meets market’s requirement for a migration path based on pre-existing installed base
slides.text:
TKIP appears to provide weak but genuine security
External review by Ron
Rivest
, David Wagner, John Kelsey, Susan Langford, and others
TKIP meets goal of software deployment on almost all existing equipment
Does not appear to  significantly degrade performance over WEP
Meets market’s requirement for a migration path based on pre-existing installed base
TKIP Summary

slides.title: Pre-Shared Key Mode
lines: “Shared Secret” is used for authentication to access point
Management is handled on the AP
Uses RADIUS protocols for authentication and key distribution
How to Negotiate a Passphrase and PTK?
slides.text:
Pre-Shared Key Mode
No RADIUS server required
“Shared Secret” is used for authentication to access point
Management is handled on the AP
Vulnerable to dictionary attacks
Enterprise Mode
Requires an authentication server
Uses RADIUS protocols for authentication and key distribution
Centralizes management of user credentials
How to Negotiate a Passphrase and PTK?

slides.title: For home / SOHO use
lines: Significantly increases the effort required to allow passive monitoring and decrypting of traffic
Also generates the secret session keys (PTK)
No standardized way of changing shared secret
If shared secret becomes known, network security may be compromised
The more complex the shared secret, the less likely it will fall to dictionary attacks
slides.text:
For home / SOHO use
Significantly increases the effort required to allow passive monitoring and decrypting of traffic
Adopts
4-Way Handshake Authentication
Also generates the secret session keys (PTK)
Pre-shared Key + TKIP
No standardized way of changing shared secret
If shared secret becomes known, network security may be compromised
Weak against passive dictionary attack
The more complex the shared secret, the less likely it will fall to dictionary attacks
WPA-PSK Overview

slides.title: How much time it will take to crack a simple passphrase with only alphanumerical values (upper and lower case)
lines: How much time it will take to crack a simple passphrase with only alphanumerical values (upper and lower case)
slides.text:
How much time it will take to crack a simple passphrase with only alphanumerical values (upper and lower case)
Smallest WPA passphrase: 8 characters (max 63)
62 possibilities per character: [A-Z][a-z][0-9]
62^8 = 218 340 105 584 896 possible keys
Using a 280GTX processor (11000 keys/sec)
218340105584896/11000k/s= 19 849 100 508 sec
19849100508 sec = 5 513 639 hours
5513639 hours = 229 735 days
229735 days = 630 years
WPA-PSK: the Brute Force Attack

slides.title: A bit too long for a simple passphrase
lines: A bit too long for a simple passphrase
For a 12 character long passphrase, brute force will take 9 309 091 680 years.
slides.text:
A bit too long for a simple passphrase
For a 12 character long passphrase, brute force will take 9 309 091 680 years.
Dictionary attack
is
still the better solution.
630 Years for an 8 Character Key
The better solution

slides.title: Rainbow tables are indexed hash lists
lines: Rainbow tables are indexed hash lists
Pre-hash millions of words against top SSID's
Known tables exist for up to 14 characters
Cracks WPA v1 and v2 with pre-shared key
slides.text:
Rainbow tables are indexed hash lists
Pre-hash millions of words against top SSID's
Required 2-3 TB of space
Known tables exist for up to 14 characters
Cracks WPA v1 and v2 with pre-shared key
Drastically faster checking onsite
WPA-PSK: Rainbow Table

slides.title: WPA-PSK should be used with care
lines: WPA-PSK should be used with care
Vulnerable to external dictionary attacks, if short
No effective attacks found so far
slides.text:
WPA-PSK should be used with care
Vulnerable to external dictionary attacks, if short
Choose WPA + 802.1X
No effective attacks found so far
Suggestions for WPA-PSK

slides.title: Authentication server distributes different keys to each user using 802.1X.
lines: Authentication server distributes different keys to each user using 802.1X.
Preferred for enterprise mode - business, government, education
slides.text:
Authentication server distributes different keys to each user using 802.1X.
Provide enhanced security and authentication
Preferred for enterprise mode - business, government, education
WPA Enterprise Mode

slides.title: 802.1X is an IEEE standard for port-based network access control for LANs
lines: 802.1X is an IEEE standard for port-based network access control for LANs
It fulfills the security loopholes of access control, authentication and key management in the 802.11 architecture
The authentication is usually done by a third-party entity, such as a RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial In User Service) server
slides.text:
802.1X is an IEEE standard for port-based network access control for LANs
It fulfills the security loopholes of access control, authentication and key management in the 802.11 architecture
The authentication is usually done by a third-party entity, such as a RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial In User Service) server
IEEE 802.1X

A carrier protocol designed to transport the messages of “real” authentication protocols (e.g., TLS)
It is a generic (flexible) authentication protocol  that can run over any link layer protocol
It Provides some common functions and a negotiation of the desired authentication mechanism called methods.
Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP)

slides.title: EAP is very simple, only four types of messages:
lines: EAP is very simple, only four types of messages:
EAP request – carries messages from AS to M
EAP response – carries messages from M to the AS
uthenticator (AP) doesn’t understand what is inside the EAP messages, it recognizes only EAP success and failure
EAP is not an authentication method itself
slides.text:
EAP is very simple, only four types of messages:
EAP request – carries messages from AS to M
EAP response – carries messages from M to the AS
EAP success – signals successful authentication
EAP failure – signals authentication failure
A
uthenticator (AP) doesn’t understand what is inside the EAP messages, it recognizes only EAP success and failure
EAP is not an authentication method itself
Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP)

slides.title: At the end of authentication:
lines: The authentication server and client have established a session
The authentication server and client possess a mutually authenticated Master Key (derived from the concrete EAP method)
Master Key represents decision to grant access based on authentication
Client and authentication server have derived PMK (Pairwise Master Key)
PMK is an authorization token to enforce access control decision at AP
Authentication server has distributed PMK to an AP

PMK is an authorization token to enforce access control decision at AP

Normally, in WPA, a falsified encrypted packet that is made from an encrypted packet accepted in the past
is discarded
since the value of IV is checked.
However, the
chopchop
attack can be executed on WPA implementations those support
IEEE802.11e
QoS
features
, which allow
8 different channels
for different data flows, and each channel has the
TSC counter independently
.
The attacker can capture an encrypted packet from one channel and execute the
chopchop
attack on
another channel
that has a smaller TSC counter.
The Beck-
Tews
attack

full_text:
Lecture 10: Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA)
Instructor:
Kui
Ren
Wireless network and IoT security

Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA) is
A response by the WLAN industry to offer an immediate, a stronger security solution than WEP.
WPA is intended to be:
A software/firmware upgrade to existing access points
Inexpensive in terms of time and cost to implement
Compatible with vendors
Suitable for enterprise, small sites, home networks
Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA)

WPA wraps RC4 cipher engine in four new algorithms
A Message Integrity Code (MIC) called Michael
Designed for deployed hardware
Requires use of active countermeasures
Extended 48-bit IV and IV Sequencing Rules
2
48
is a large number! More than 500 trillion
Sequencing rules specify how IVs are selected and verified
Key Derivation and Distribution
Creating a key hierarchy out of the master key
Master keys can be refreshed in a secure manner
Temporal Key Integrity Protocol generates per-packet keys
How WPA Addresses the WEP Vulnerabilities

Encryption method:
RC4
Key size:
128 bits (varies)
Changes encryption key every frame
Key management:
TKIP
Resolves keys to be used, looks at client’s configuration;
Sets unique default key for each client
Hash method:
Michael
8 bytes, placed between data and ICV
802.1x authentication:
can be required
Key WPA Facts

Message Integrity Code (MIC) - 64-bit message calculated using the “Michael” algorithm
Designed for deployed legacy hardware
The Michael algorithm was the strongest that WPA designers could come up with that would still work with most older network cards
Uses a lot swaps, shifts, even discarding operations
Protects both data and header
The MIC is a hash that is calculated on a per-packet basis
The MIC is also on a per-sender, per-receiver basis
The MIC is based on seed value (IVs), destination MAC, source MAC, and payload
Message Integrity Check: Michael Algorithm

Data Integrity: The Michael MIC Algorithm
Destination MAC
Source MAC
802.11 Data Payload
Michael
64 Bit MIC
Data Payload
16 Bit IV
64 Bit
MIC
32 Bit
ICV
Per Frame
Encryption
Frame Transmitted

Due to inevitable weaknesses of Michael, WPA implements
a 16-bit monotonically incrementing counter
that discourages replay attacks:
Take the 1st and 3rd bytes of the old IV
Reset packet sequence # to 0 on rekey
Increment sequence # by 1 on each packet
Drop any packet received out of sequence
Defeating Replays: IV Sequence Enforcement

Wireless Station

Refers to a process of delivering fresh encryption and integrity keys (MIC Keys) to the stations and Aps.
WPA Key hierarchy consists of a master key and session keys
Master key, called the
Pairwise Master Key
(PMK), is derived from either an 802.1X key exchange or from the passphrase
Session keys, collectively called the
Pairwise Transient Key
(PTK), are derived from the master key
TKIP Re-Key Mechanism

Pairwise

(Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2 (PBKDF2)

Pairwise Transient Key (PTK)

WEP
constructs a per-packet key by simply concatenating a base-key and the IV
TKIP
constructs a per-packet key by going through 2 key mixing phases
The mixing phases make difficult for an attacker to correlate IVs and per-packet key
TKIP:
Per-Packet Key Mixing
Phase 1
Phase 2
Temporal Key
MAC

Sequence Number
Per-packet key

TKIP Encryption Process

TKIP Decryption Process

TKIP appears to provide weak but genuine security
External review by Ron
Rivest
, David Wagner, John Kelsey, Susan Langford, and others
TKIP meets goal of software deployment on almost all existing equipment
Does not appear to  significantly degrade performance over WEP
Meets market’s requirement for a migration path based on pre-existing installed base
TKIP Summary

Pre-Shared Key Mode
No RADIUS server required
“Shared Secret” is used for authentication to access point
Management is handled on the AP
Vulnerable to dictionary attacks
Enterprise Mode
Requires an authentication server
Uses RADIUS protocols for authentication and key distribution
Centralizes management of user credentials
How to Negotiate a Passphrase and PTK?

For home / SOHO use
Significantly increases the effort required to allow passive monitoring and decrypting of traffic
Adopts
4-Way Handshake Authentication
Also generates the secret session keys (PTK)
Pre-shared Key + TKIP
No standardized way of changing shared secret
If shared secret becomes known, network security may be compromised
Weak against passive dictionary attack
The more complex the shared secret, the less likely it will fall to dictionary attacks
WPA-PSK Overview

The Role of 4-Way Handshake:  Authentication and Key Exchange

PSK: 4-Way
Handshake
Authentication

4-way handshake protected with PTK
The strenght of PTK relies on the strength of the password
PSK = PBKDF2 (Password, SSID,
SSIDlength
, 4096, 256)
Offline brute-force
and
dictionary attacks
possible
Attacker captures (records) 4-way handshake messages
Attacker performs brute-force or dictionary attacks (at home)
Vulnerabilities of 4-way handshake

How much time it will take to crack a simple passphrase with only alphanumerical values (upper and lower case)
Smallest WPA passphrase: 8 characters (max 63)
62 possibilities per character: [A-Z][a-z][0-9]
62^8 = 218 340 105 584 896 possible keys
Using a 280GTX processor (11000 keys/sec)
218340105584896/11000k/s= 19 849 100 508 sec
19849100508 sec = 5 513 639 hours
5513639 hours = 229 735 days
229735 days = 630 years
WPA-PSK: the Brute Force Attack

A bit too long for a simple passphrase
For a 12 character long passphrase, brute force will take 9 309 091 680 years.
Dictionary attack
is
still the better solution.
630 Years for an 8 Character Key
The better solution

Rainbow tables are indexed hash lists
Pre-hash millions of words against top SSID's
Required 2-3 TB of space
Known tables exist for up to 14 characters
Cracks WPA v1 and v2 with pre-shared key
Drastically faster checking onsite
WPA-PSK: Rainbow Table

WPA-PSK should be used with care
Vulnerable to external dictionary attacks, if short
Choose WPA + 802.1X
No effective attacks found so far
Suggestions for WPA-PSK

Authentication server distributes different keys to each user using 802.1X.
Provide enhanced security and authentication
Preferred for enterprise mode - business, government, education
WPA Enterprise Mode

802.1X is an IEEE standard for port-based network access control for LANs
It fulfills the security loopholes of access control, authentication and key management in the 802.11 architecture
The authentication is usually done by a third-party entity, such as a RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial In User Service) server
IEEE 802.1X

Main 802.1X components
Supplicant (e.g., a wireless client)
Authenticator (e.g., an Access Point)
Authentication Server (e.g., a RADIUS server)

B
ased
on
EAP
(Extensible Authentication Protocol)
and the specific authentication method agreed in the association phase.
802.1X
Authentication

A carrier protocol designed to transport the messages of “real” authentication protocols (e.g., TLS)
It is a generic (flexible) authentication protocol  that can run over any link layer protocol
It Provides some common functions and a negotiation of the desired authentication mechanism called methods.
Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP)

EAP is very simple, only four types of messages:
EAP request – carries messages from AS to M
EAP response – carries messages from M to the AS
EAP success – signals successful authentication
EAP failure – signals authentication failure
A
uthenticator (AP) doesn’t understand what is inside the EAP messages, it recognizes only EAP success and failure
EAP is not an authentication method itself
Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP)

End-to-end transport between client and authentication server
AP proxies EAP between 802.1X and backend protocol between AP and AS (e.g. RADIUS)

At the end of authentication:
The authentication server and client have established a session
The authentication server and client possess a mutually authenticated Master Key (derived from the concrete EAP method)
Master Key represents decision to grant access based on authentication
Client and authentication server have derived PMK (Pairwise Master Key)
PMK is an authorization token to enforce access control decision at AP
Authentication server has distributed PMK to an AP
IEEE 802.1X authentication summary

Normally, in WPA, a falsified encrypted packet that is made from an encrypted packet accepted in the past
is discarded
since the value of IV is checked.
However, the
chopchop
attack can be executed on WPA implementations those support
IEEE802.11e
QoS
features
, which allow
8 different channels
for different data flows, and each channel has the
TSC counter independently
.
The attacker can capture an encrypted packet from one channel and execute the
chopchop
attack on
another channel
that has a smaller TSC counter.
